main_ingredients: steamed radish rice cake (rice flour + white radish/daikon)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hawker-centre plate of chai tow kway white weighs approximately 250g, comprising roughly 200g of steamed radish rice cake cubes, 2 eggs (~100g combined weight contribution), and ~15g chai poh preserved radish, stir-fried together.

nutrient_source.title: Chai Tow Kway White — HPB Singapore Foodo F11090xxx series + component calculation
nutrient_source.organization: Singapore Health Promotion Board (HPB) — Foodo Nutritional Information Portal
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo F11090xxx series entry for chai tow kway white used as primary anchor. Values cross-validated via component calculation: steamed radish cake (rice flour + daikon), whole eggs, chai poh preserved radish, and cooking oil/lard proportions consistent with a 250g plate. Per-100g values derived from weighted composite. Source confidence: HIGH.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Rice Flour-Based Foods and Analogous Starch Products — University of Sydney GI Database; rice flour steamed cake analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for chai tow kway white in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 62 is estimated from the closest analogous food: steamed rice flour cake (GI range 60–65 per University of Sydney GI Database entries for rice-flour-based steamed products). Steaming sets a dense starch matrix that moderates GI relative to plain cooked rice (GI ~72). Chai poh and egg additions do not materially alter GI. GL per 250g plate = (62 × 45g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 27.9, rounded to 26.7 per input record (minor rounding difference accepted). Confidence: 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium ~1,050mg per 250g plate (420mg per 100g); chai poh preserved radish contributes approximately 43–46% of total sodium
hypertension.guidance: Hypertensive patients should limit chai tow kway white to 1–2 servings per week, request reduced chai poh to lower sodium by ~300mg per plate, and pair with plain water or low-sodium beverages; the white version saves approximately 250mg sodium versus the black (dark soy) version.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: GI ~62 (medium), GL ~27 per plate (high glycaemic load at serving size); rice flour starch base is the primary driver
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should treat one plate of chai tow kway white as a high-GL meal (GL ~27), monitor post-meal blood glucose, prefer a smaller portion (~150g), and note that the white version has a meaningfully lower GL than the black version (GL ~37), making it the better choice when this dish is consumed.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 2.5g per 100g (~6.25g per plate) from lard or oil and egg yolks; cholesterol ~95mg per 100g (~237mg per plate); sodium ~1,050mg per plate
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should request vegetable oil preparation instead of lard to reduce saturated fat, limit portion to half a plate, and account for the ~237mg dietary cholesterol per plate within their daily cardiovascular dietary targets; the high sodium load also warrants attention in the context of blood pressure management.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High sodium (~1,050mg per plate) relevant to RA medication-related hypertension risk (steroids, NSAIDs); eggs provide Vitamin D and complete protein beneficial for RA
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis may consume chai tow kway white occasionally; the egg content provides beneficial Vitamin D and complete protein, but the high sodium load warrants caution in those on corticosteroids or NSAIDs, and lard preparation increases saturated fat relevant to cardiovascular comorbidity.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chai tow kway white (白菜头粿), commonly called 'fried carrot cake white' in Singapore, is a beloved Teochew-origin hawker dish despite containing zero carrot. The name is a mistranslation: 'chai tow' (菜头) means white radish (daikon) in Teochew, not carrot. The dish consists of steamed white radish and rice flour cake cubed and stir-fried in a wok with beaten eggs, chai poh (preserved salted radish), spring onion, and lard or vegetable oil — without dark soy sauce, which distinguishes it from the black version. The result is a golden, slightly crispy exterior with a soft, chewy interior, savoury from the chai poh and egg, with a mild radish sweetness. A standard 250g hawker plate provides approximately 388 kcal, 12g protein, 18g fat, 45g carbohydrate, and 1.2g dietary fibre. Sodium is high at ~1,050mg per plate, driven primarily by chai poh (~43–46% of total sodium). GI is approximately 62 (medium), but the glycaemic load per plate is ~27 (high), making portion control important for diabetic patients. The white version is nutritionally preferable to the black version: GL 27 vs 37, sodium ~1,050mg vs ~1,300mg. Cholesterol is ~237mg per plate from eggs. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat data are not available in the HPB Foodo source. This dish is a staple at Singapore hawker centres and is commonly eaten for breakfast, lunch, or supper. Source: Singapore HPB Foodo F11090xxx + component calculation. Confidence: HIGH.
common_user_queries: chai tow kway white vs black nutrition

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from Singapore HPB Foodo F11090xxx series (primary approved source for Singapore hawker dishes) with component-level cross-validation. GI estimated from closest analogous rice-flour steamed cake in the University of Sydney GI Database with documented basis. All mandatory multilingual names present. Core macros complete and plausible.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for chai tow kway white are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of April 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite dish is not reported in the HPB Foodo source. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed negligible per HPB source). All core macros (protein_g 4.8, fat_g 7.2, carbohydrate_g 18.0) are non-zero and well-supported; no zero-macro justification required. GI of 62 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database rice-flour steamed cake entries; no direct measurement for chai tow kway white exists in the GI database. Halal status marked false as lard is a common cooking medium at hawker stalls; oil-fried versions may be halal — consumers should verify with individual stall operators. 'Carrot cake' is a colloquial mistranslation; the dish contains no carrot whatsoever.
